nameMeanings.name: Masters
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: habitational and occupational name from Middle English (atte) maisteres ‘(at the) master's (house)’, a name for someone who lived or worked there, normally a servant. Compare Master and Masterman , and for the type compare Maidens , Monks , Parsons .
variant of Master , with post-medieval excrescent -s.

variants.name: Master
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Amar, Dhimant, Pravin, Amit, Aruna, Bhanu, Gaurang, Gopal, Haren, Jagu, Jayesh, Kalpesh.

English: occupational name from Middle English maister ‘master’ (Old French maistre). The word had a wide range of applications, and the reference in surnames may be to a schoolmaster, to the master of a farm or house, to the master of an apprentice, or to a ship's captain. In Scotland the eldest sons of barons and the uncles of lords were called Masters, but this is not primarily a Scottish surname.
German: dialect form of   Meister   and, in North America, (also) an altered form of this.

variants.name: Maidens
meanings: occupational or habitational name from Middle English (atte) maydenes ‘(at the) maiden's or maidens' (house)’, a name for someone who lived or worked at such a house. Compare Masters and Parsons .
post-medieval variant of Maiden , with excrescent -s.

variants.name: Marsters
meanings: English: variant of Masters . Marsters is the predominant surname on Palmerston Island, Cook Islands, South Pacific, deriving from a settler and owner reputedly born in Leicestershire.

nameMeanings.name: Joan
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Contracted form of Old French Jo(h)anne, from Latin Io(h)anna ( see Joanna ). In England this was the usual feminine form of John from the Middle English period onwards and was extremely popular, but in the 16th and 17th centuries it steadily lost ground to Jane . It was strongly revived in the first part of the 20th century, partly under the influence of George Bernard Shaw's play St Joan ( 1923 ), based on the life of Joan of Arc ( 1412–31 ). Claiming to be guided by the voices of the saints, she persuaded the French dauphin to defy the occupying English forces and have himself crowned, and she led the French army that raised the siege of Orleans in 1429 . The following year she was captured by the Burgundians and sold to the English, and a year later she was burned at the stake for witchcraft at the age of 18 or 19. Her story has captured the imagination of many writers, and she is variously portrayed as a national and political hero, a model of apolitical straightforwardness and honesty, and a religious heroine. She was canonized in 1920 . More recent influences have included the American film actress Joan Crawford ( 1908–77 , born Lucille le Sueur ), the British actress Joan Collins ( b. 1933 ), the American comedienne Joan Rivers ( b. 1933 ), and the West Indian pop singer Joan Armatrading ( b. 1950 ).

nameMeanings.name: Anne
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Pet form of   Ann   or   Anne .

variants.name: Anni
meanings: Pet form of   Ann   or   Anne .

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

historicEvents.title: The First English Colony is Established in America
historicEvents.description: England established its first colony in North America in 1585 as a way to generate additional wealth. The colony was named Virginia, after the virgin Queen Elizabeth I.

en.lifeSummary: When Joan Anne Masters was born in 1523, in Tintinhull, Somerset, England, her father, John Masters, was 20 and her mother, Mrs John Masters, was 20. She married James Napier of Punknoll in 1544, in Tintinhull, Somerset, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 5 sons and 2 daughters. She died on 27 June 1586, in London, England, at the age of 63.

ko.lifeSummary: Joan Anne Masters(이)가 1523년 영국 서머세트 틴틴훌에서 태어났을 때, 그녀의 아버지 John Masters(은)는 20세였고, 그녀의 어머니 Mrs John Masters(은)는 20세였다. 그녀는 1544년 영국 잉글랜드 서머세트 틴틴훌에서 James Napier of Punknoll(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 다섯 명의 아들과 두 명의 딸을 두었다. 그녀는 1586년 6월 27일 영국 런던에서 63세의 나이로 사망했다.

Она вышла замуж за человека по имени James Napier of Punknoll в 1544 г. в местности тинтинулл, сомирсет, Англия, Великобритания. У них было по крайней мере 5 сыновей и 2 дочери. Она умерла 27 июня 1586 г. в местности Лондон, Англия в возрасте 63 лет.
